title: ANSSI Hygiene Principles
content: Security hardening relies on patching discipline, least privilege, segmentation, and monitored administration channels.

title: CERT-FR Incident Triage
content: Incident triage should quickly determine scope, affected assets, and probable entry points, then preserve evidence before remediation actions.

title: CISA KEV Prioritization
content: When a CVE is listed in CISA KEV, remediation priority is typically higher because exploitation has been observed in the wild.

title: IANA Port 20
content: Port 20 is commonly associated with 'ftp-data' over tcp. Analysts should confirm with the current IANA registry and packet/session context before concluding service identity.

title: IP Protocol Number 1
content: IANA protocol number 1 corresponds to ICMP. This field is used in IP headers to indicate the next encapsulated protocol.

title: IP Protocol Number 2
content: IANA protocol number 2 corresponds to IGMP. This field is used in IP headers to indicate the next encapsulated protocol.

title: IP Protocol Number 6
content: IANA protocol number 6 corresponds to TCP. This field is used in IP headers to indicate the next encapsulated protocol.

title: IP Protocol Number 17
content: IANA protocol number 17 corresponds to UDP. This field is used in IP headers to indicate the next encapsulated protocol.

title: IP Protocol Number 41
content: IANA protocol number 41 corresponds to IPv6. This field is used in IP headers to indicate the next encapsulated protocol.

title: IP Protocol Number 47
content: IANA protocol number 47 corresponds to GRE. This field is used in IP headers to indicate the next encapsulated protocol.

title: IP Protocol Number 50
content: IANA protocol number 50 corresponds to ESP. This field is used in IP headers to indicate the next encapsulated protocol.

title: IP Protocol Number 51
content: IANA protocol number 51 corresponds to AH. This field is used in IP headers to indicate the next encapsulated protocol.

title: IP Protocol Number 58
content: IANA protocol number 58 corresponds to ICMPv6. This field is used in IP headers to indicate the next encapsulated protocol.

title: IP Protocol Number 89
content: IANA protocol number 89 corresponds to OSPF. This field is used in IP headers to indicate the next encapsulated protocol.

title: IP Protocol Number 132
content: IANA protocol number 132 corresponds to SCTP. This field is used in IP headers to indicate the next encapsulated protocol.

title: T1001 Data Obfuscation
content: C2 payloads can be obfuscated to reduce signature-based detection effectiveness.

title: T1016 System Network Configuration Discovery
content: Collection of local network configuration can guide target selection and routing-aware movement.

title: T1021 Remote Services
content: Remote service protocols are frequently used for persistence and lateral movement once credentials are obtained.

title: T1040 Network Sniffing
content: Traffic capture by adversaries can expose credentials and operational context for follow-on actions.

title: T1041 Exfiltration Over C2 Channel
content: Exfiltration may occur inside established command-and-control channels to reduce detection likelihood.

title: T1046 Network Service Discovery
content: Scanning services and ports on remote systems is a common precursor to exploitation and lateral movement.

title: T1071 Application Layer Protocol
content: Adversaries can use common application protocols to blend command-and-control traffic with normal operations.

title: T1071.001 Web Protocols
content: HTTP(S)-based C2 can resemble legitimate web traffic; sequence, destinations, and timing become critical evidence.

title: T1071.004 DNS
content: DNS can be abused for command, control, or data transport through atypical query patterns and volumes.

title: T1090 Proxy
content: Proxy infrastructure can obscure final C2 destinations and complicate attribution.

title: T1110 Brute Force
content: Repeated authentication attempts over management and remote access protocols can indicate credential guessing activity.

title: T1133 External Remote Services
content: Adversaries can access internet-facing remote services to gain initial foothold or maintain access.

title: T1498 Network Denial of Service
content: Sustained abnormal packet rates against services can indicate resource exhaustion attempts.

title: T1568 Dynamic Resolution
content: Frequent endpoint resolution changes can support resilient malicious infrastructure and evasive operations.

title: T1571 Non-Standard Port
content: Use of unexpected ports for known protocols can be an evasion indicator requiring context-based validation.

title: T1573 Encrypted Channel
content: Encrypted channels protect adversary traffic content and force defenders to rely on metadata and behavior.

title: T1595 Active Scanning
content: Pre-compromise active scanning can identify reachable assets, versions, and service surfaces.

title: NVD CVSS Interpretation
content: CVSS helps rank technical severity, but operational risk also depends on exploitability, exposure, and asset criticality.

title: OWASP Logging Guidance
content: Security logs should be structured, timestamped, and correlated across systems to support rapid detection and post-incident analysis.

title: Domain Names - Concepts and Facilities
content: DNS separates recursive resolution and authoritative answers. Caching and delegation are central mechanisms for scalable name resolution.

title: Domain Names - Implementation and Specification
content: DNS messages include header flags, question section, and resource-record sections. UDP is common; TCP is used for large responses and specific operations.

title: Requirements for IP Version 4 Routers
content: IPv4 routers decrement TTL and discard expired packets, typically generating ICMP Time Exceeded messages under normal control-plane behavior.

title: Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol
content: DHCPv4 typically uses UDP ports 67 and 68 for discover, offer, request, and acknowledgment exchange during host configuration.

title: SSH Transport Layer Protocol
content: SSH establishes an encrypted and integrity-protected transport channel, typically over TCP port 22, with host key verification and rekey support.

title: Security Architecture for IP
content: IPsec uses ESP and AH for packet protection at the IP layer. Encrypted tunnels can hide upper-layer protocol details from passive observation.

title: ICMPv6
content: ICMPv6 is required for IPv6 operation and supports error signaling and diagnostics. Neighbor discovery relies on ICMPv6 message types.

title: Simple Mail Transfer Protocol
content: SMTP defines message relay and transfer commands across mail servers. Submission and relay flows often involve ports 25, 465, or 587.

title: Network Time Protocol Version 4
content: NTP synchronizes clocks over UDP port 123. Regular short periodic exchanges are normal for time discipline.

title: Computing TCP Retransmission Timer
content: TCP retransmission timeout is computed from smoothed RTT and RTT variation. Excessive retransmissions indicate potential loss, congestion, or path instability.

title: Multicast DNS
content: mDNS performs local-link DNS resolution over multicast UDP 5353, commonly used by service discovery stacks in residential networks.

title: Virtual eXtensible Local Area Network (VXLAN)
content: VXLAN encapsulates Layer 2 frames in UDP for Layer 3 overlays, usually on UDP port 4789 in virtualized environments.

title: User Datagram Protocol (UDP)
content: UDP provides source port, destination port, length, and checksum fields. It is connectionless and does not provide retransmission or in-order delivery.

title: Specification for DNS over TLS
content: DNS over TLS encapsulates DNS queries in TLS sessions to protect resolver privacy and integrity against passive observation.

title: Internet Protocol Version 4
content: IPv4 carries packets across routed networks. Routers decrement TTL by at least one per hop; a packet is discarded when TTL reaches zero.

title: Internet Control Message Protocol (ICMP)
content: ICMP provides diagnostics and control information for IPv4, including echo request/reply and error reporting such as destination unreachable and time exceeded.

title: Internet Protocol Version 6
content: IPv6 uses a fixed-size base header and Hop Limit instead of TTL. Extension headers may be chained after the base header before transport data.

title: Address Resolution Protocol (ARP)
content: ARP maps IPv4 addresses to link-layer addresses on local networks. ARP traffic is commonly seen as broadcast requests and unicast replies.

title: DHCP for IPv6
content: DHCPv6 uses UDP ports 546 (client) and 547 (server) for stateful configuration and lease management in IPv6 networks.

title: Transport Layer Security 1.3
content: TLS 1.3 encrypts most handshake messages after initial key exchange and uses authenticated encryption for application data confidentiality and integrity.

title: DNS Queries over HTTPS (DoH)
content: DoH transports DNS messages over HTTPS, commonly using TCP or QUIC underneath, making DNS traffic blend with web traffic patterns.

title: QUIC: A UDP-Based Multiplexed and Secure Transport
content: QUIC runs over UDP, integrates transport and cryptographic handshake, and supports stream multiplexing with connection migration capabilities.

title: Using TLS to Secure QUIC
content: QUIC uses TLS 1.3 handshake semantics but carries them in QUIC frames, not in traditional TLS-over-TCP records.

title: HTTP Semantics
content: HTTP semantics define method behavior, status code classes, and metadata fields independent of HTTP version framing details.

title: HTTP/1.1
content: HTTP/1.1 defines textual request and response framing. In encrypted deployments, HTTP semantics are wrapped in TLS sessions.

title: HTTP/3
content: HTTP/3 maps HTTP semantics to QUIC streams. Traffic often appears as encrypted UDP flows on port 443 with QUIC transport behavior.

title: Transmission Control Protocol (TCP)
content: TCP is a connection-oriented transport protocol. It uses sequence numbers, acknowledgments, retransmission, and flow control to provide reliable ordered delivery between endpoints.

title: Beaconing Behavioral Indicator
content: Periodic low-volume outbound traffic to a stable destination can indicate beaconing, but software updates and telemetry can produce similar periodic patterns.

title: Brute Force Indicator
content: Repeated authentication attempts over SSH, RDP, SMTP, or web login channels can indicate brute force, especially with rapid failure bursts.

title: DNS Tunneling Indicator
content: Long, high-entropy, repetitive subdomain queries with unusual frequency can indicate DNS-based data transfer or command signaling.

title: Exfiltration Indicator
content: Unusual outbound volume spikes to uncommon destinations, especially outside known business patterns, can indicate exfiltration and should trigger validation.

title: SYN Scan Indicator
content: A high number of SYN packets to many distinct targets or ports in a short window is consistent with service discovery scanning behavior.

title: Wireshark Operational Guidance
content: Normal ARP traffic is local-link scoped. Repeated unsolicited ARP replies or abrupt mapping changes may indicate spoofing risks in flat L2 segments.

title: Wireshark Operational Guidance
content: Wireshark display filters are expression-based and different from capture filters. Analysts should keep capture broad enough and refine views using display filters.

title: Wireshark Operational Guidance
content: DNS analysis relies on transaction IDs, query names, response codes, and answer sections. Repeated failures or unusual query entropy can indicate abuse patterns.

title: Wireshark Operational Guidance
content: Expert warnings should guide analyst attention but not be treated as proof. Confirmation should come from packet-level evidence and protocol semantics.

title: Wireshark Operational Guidance
content: Follow stream workflows help reconstruct request/response narratives. For encrypted traffic, metadata and timing remain key evidence when payload is unreadable.

title: Wireshark Operational Guidance
content: ICMP Time Exceeded often reflects TTL expiration on routed paths. Correlating original flow and returned ICMP payload helps isolate hop-level issues.

title: Wireshark Operational Guidance
content: Comparing ip.ttl or ipv6.hlim across packets can reveal path changes, asymmetric routing hypotheses, or unusual decrement patterns.

title: Wireshark Operational Guidance
content: A practical sanity check is verifying EtherType against decoded Layer 3 protocol. Incoherence can indicate malformed frames, parser issues, or capture corruption.

title: Wireshark Operational Guidance
content: With QUIC over UDP/443, payload content is encrypted; investigators should focus on timing, packet sizes, endpoint behavior, and flow persistence.

title: Wireshark Operational Guidance
content: SYN without ACK usually marks handshake initiation. SYN+ACK usually marks handshake response. FIN and RST indicate session close or reset.
